title: CLAUDE.md
text: Dataset access (R/tabs.R, R/data.R): Utilities filter summarize bundled datasets — woncan_types(), filter_woncan(), table_woncan(), MA_cancer_rate_table(). datasets loaded via data(name, package="YESCDS"). Maps (R/map_app.R, R/cancer_map_world.R, R/mass_map.R): Leaflet-based interactive maps US MSA-level (cancer_map_usa()), US state/county (map_app(), mass_map()), global (cancer_map_world()) cancer incidence. Circle radii scaled age-adjusted rate. Shiny apps (R/stapp.R): Launcher functions (variation_app(), cancer_map_app(), map_app()) setwd() inst/<appdir> call shiny::runApp(). actual ui.R/server.R files live inst/stapp/, inst/cancer_map/, inst/map_app/, inst/spatial_app/. Survival analysis (R/survcode.R, R/build_surv.R): Kaplan–Meier curve helpers (build_simple_survival_curve(), show_median_estimate(), show_5y_estimate(), do_new_surv()), build_surv_for_mut() pulls TCGA data via curatedTCGAData returns list surv, coldata, mutdata mutation-stratified KM analysis. Spatial transcriptomics (inst/spatial_app/, R/cache_hcc.R): Shiny app displays H&E images Visium spot assignments two HCC samples (“1R” responder, “6NR” non-responder). Large .rda files fetched OSN (mghp.osn.xsede.org) cached locally via BiocFileCache get_hcc_spatial_path(). Visualization (R/viz.R, R/CIplots.R): make_hist(), plotwci(), make_comparison(), compare_tumors(), ordered_seg_cal() (ordered CI segments CA lung cancer data). Teaching utilities (R/poker.R, R/build_deck.R): Card game helpers (build_deck(), faces(), suits(), one_pair(), two_pairs(), full_house(), is_flush()) used probability exercises.

what: YESCDS: Cancer Data Science for YES for CURE
title: YESCDS: Cancer Data Science for YES for CURE
text: YESCDS R/Bioconductor package provides code, data, interactive tools teaching cancer data science secondary undergraduate students. developed support Dana-Farber Cancer Institute (DFCI) CZI YES CURE collaboration — program designed introduce young people cancer research careers.

title: YESCDS: Cancer Data Science for YES for CURE
text: package structured self-contained curriculum. Students explore real public health datasets, build intuitions cancer epidemiology, practice reading interactive visualizations, get first look genomic data science — within R. prior programming experience assumed early modules.

title: YESCDS: Cancer Data Science for YES for CURE
text: vignettes organized five thematic modules, labeled E:

title: YESCDS: Cancer Data Science for YES for CURE
text: YESCDS ships curated, ready--use datasets students can start exploring immediately: SEER_2018 — age-adjusted US cancer incidence rates NCI SEER (18 registry areas) woncan / woncan_meta — cancer rates US Metropolitan Statistical Area (1999–2018) CDC WONDER world_cancer_2020_all + world_geo_sf — global 2020 incidence data merged country boundaries mapping us_county_geo — US county spatial polygons (sf format) metabric — clinical features METABRIC breast cancer cohort (cBioPortal) prostate_met_adeno — metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma clinical data brcamutSE — BRCA mutation calls TCGA SummarizedExperiment container canada_crude — crude Canadian cancer rates Statistics Canada vjc_cancer_net — small illustrative dataset personal cancer contacts

title: YESCDS: Cancer Data Science for YES for CURE
text: Several Shiny apps bundled hands-exploration: cancer_map_app() / cancer_map_usa() / cancer_map_world() — leaflet-based maps US global cancer incidence; students can select cancer type year interactively map_app() / mass_map() — Massachusetts county cancer rate explorer variation_app() — interactive survival curve comparison across cancer subtypes Spatial transcriptomics app (inst/spatial_app/) — displays H&E images, Visium spot assignments, gene expression hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) samples; introduces students spatial genomics

title: YESCDS: Cancer Data Science for YES for CURE
text: R ≥ 4.0, distributed via Bioconductor (biocViews: Cancer) Interactive visualizations: shiny, leaflet, plotly, ggplot2 Geospatial: sf (simple features) Genomics: SummarizedExperiment, curatedTCGAData, TCGAutils Survival analysis: survival

title: YESCDS: Cancer Data Science for YES for CURE
text: Contributors include faculty staff Channing Division Network Medicine (Mass General Brigham), Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Perelman School Medicine (University Pennsylvania). project supported NSF ACCESS, Chan-Zuckerberg Initiative EOSS Diversity program, NCI (5U24CA180996), NHGRI (2U24HG004059). Source issue tracker: https://github.com/vjcitn/YESCDS

title: D4 Methods of comparing treatments
text: Abstract 1979 paper:
code: D.4.1 Treatment of patients with advanced ovarian carcinoma (stages IIIB and IV) using  either cyclophosphamide alone (1 g/m2) or cyclophosphamide (500 mg/m2) plus  adriamycin (40 mg/m2) by iv injection every 3 weeks each produced partial  regression in approximately one third of the patients. Survival curves and  time-to-progression curves for the two regimens were nearly identical  in these patients with advanced disease. These same regimens produced different  results when used monthly in patients who had minimal residual  disease (stages II and IIIA). In patients with minimal residual disease  the therapeutic index of the combination regimen was superior to that of  cyclophosphamide alone. Prognosis was better overall among patients with  minimal residual disease than among patients with advanced disease. Within  the minimal-disease group grossly complete excision of tumor prior to  chemotherapy was associated with still better prognosis. Among patients  with advanced disease, prognosis was significantly better for older patients  despite their generally less favorable performance scores. Much  of this prognostic superiority appeared to be related to menopausal  status and presumably to the depletion of endogenous estrogens in the older patients.

code: nouns = c("Mercedes", "Tiffany", "Lamborghini", "Maserati", "Tosca", "La Traviata",    "Diamonds", "Crystal", "Lenox") pastverbs = c("radiated", "glowed", "luxuriated", "ecstatized", "overwhelmed",   "hyperventilated", "raptured", "raved", "stupendousized") preps = c("on", "over", "beyond", "above", "beneath", "within", "across") adjectives = c("magnificent", "majestic", "sophisticated", "exquisite",   "glorious", "regal") preps = c("on", "over", "beyond", "above", "beneath", "within", "across") indic = c("the", "a", "any", "some") (*) indicative + noun + verb(tensed) + preposition + indicative + adjective + noun get1 = function (x) sample(x, size=1) up1 = function(x) paste0(toupper(substring(x,1,1)), substring(x,2)) sentence = function() {   cat(sprintf("%s %s %s %s %s %s %s.", up1(get1(indic)), get1(nouns), get1(pastverbs),      get1(preps), get1(indic), get1(adjectives), get1(nouns))) } set.seed(12345) # reproducible sentence() ## A Lamborghini raved over the sophisticated Crystal. sentence() ## A La Traviata hyperventilated across a majestic Mercedes. sentence() ## Some Crystal raptured within a magnificent Maserati. sentence() ## Some Lamborghini stupendousized within the exquisite Diamonds. sentence_nr = function() {   i1 = get1(indic)   n1 = get1(nouns)    pv1 = get1(pastverbs)   p1 = get1(preps)   i2 = get1(setdiff(indic,i1))   # don't repeat indicative   ad = get1(adjectives)    n2 = get1(setdiff(nouns,n1)) # don't repeat noun   sprintf("%s %s %s %s %s %s %s.", up1(i1), n1, pv1, p1, i2, ad, n2) } sentence_nr() ## [1] "Some Tiffany ecstatized beyond the magnificent Tosca." sentence_nr() ## [1] "Some La Traviata stupendousized beneath any sophisticated Mercedes." linked = function() cat(sprintf("%s,\n because %s", sub(".$", "", sentence_nr()), sentence_nr())) linked() ## Any Mercedes overwhelmed over a sophisticated Maserati, ##  because The Lamborghini radiated over some magnificent Lenox. linked() ## Any Maserati glowed above some glorious Diamonds, ##  because Any Diamonds raptured across a glorious La Traviata. noun + "is not" + noun altsentence = function() {    sprintf("%s is not %s", get1(nouns), get1(nouns)) } altsentence() ## [1] "Diamonds is not Crystal" altsentence() ## [1] "Lamborghini is not Tosca"
